Hypothesis Tests for One Variance
Introduction
A Concrete Example
Conducting One-Tailed Hypothesis Tests for Variance
Conducting Two-Tailed Hypothesis Tests for Variance
Conducting Hypothesis Tests for Variance: Applications
Prerequisites
The Chi-Square Distribution
Hypothesis Tests for One Mean: Known Population Variance
The Sample Variance
Introduction
?

In this lesson, we will discuss the sampling distribution of the sample variance and explain how this can be used to conduct hypothesis tests for the population variance

First, consider a random sample of size The sample variance defined as

is an unbiased estimate of the population variance

In general, the distribution of is difficult to find. However, if the random sample is taken from a normally distributed population, that is

then it can be shown that the sampling distribution of the random variable

follows a chi-square distribution with degrees of freedom.

Let's see how we can use this information to conduct a hypothesis test for the variance

A Concrete Example
?

Consider a sample of size from a normal population, where an unbiased estimate for the population variance obtained from this sample is We wish to carry out a hypothesis test at a significance level to determine whether there is sufficient evidence that the population variance

We are told that the population is normally distributed, but we do not know the population variance In cases like this, the random variable

follows a chi-square distribution with degrees of freedom, where

  • is the sample size,

  • is the sample variance, and

  • is the population variance.

In our example:

  • is the null hypothesis

  • is the alternative (one-tailed) hypothesis

Assuming the null hypothesis, i.e., we compute the following test statistic:

Next, we find the critical region. The right-tailed critical region is the set where

The table below shows the part of the percentage points table for the chi-square distribution for particular probabilities and degrees of freedom

The number of degrees of freedom for this test is

From the percentage points table of the chi-square distribution, we obtain that So, our critical region is

Our test statistic () lies in the critical region, as shown below.

So, we reject the null hypothesis As a result, we conclude the following:

There is evidence that, at the level of significance, we have

Let's now see an example of a left-tailed test.

Example: Conducting One-Tailed Hypothesis Tests for Variance
?

Consider a sample of size from a normal population with the following sample data:

Conduct a hypothesis test at a significance level to determine whether there is sufficient evidence that the population variance is smaller than

The table below shows the values such that for some particular values of and where follows a chi-square distribution with degrees of freedom.

EXPLANATION

We are told that the population is normally distributed, but we do not know the population variance In cases like this, the random variable

follows a chi-square distribution with degrees of freedom, where

  • is the sample size,

  • is the sample variance, and

  • is the population variance.

In our example:

  • is the null hypothesis

  • is the alternative (one-tailed) hypothesis

First, we calculate an unbiased estimate of the population variance:

Assuming the null hypothesis, i.e., we compute the test statistic:

Next, we find the critical region.

Since the alternative hypothesis is , we must consider the left tail. This means we need to find the value such that Since then according to the table, the left-tailed critical value for a chi-square random variable with degrees of freedom is

So, our critical region is Our test statistic () lies outside the critical region, as shown below.

So, we do not reject the null hypothesis As a result, we conclude the following:

There is evidence that, at the level of significance, we have

KEY PREREQUISITES
The Chi-Square Distribution
Hypothesis Tests for One Mean: Known Population Variance
The Sample Variance
Question 1
?
A scientific calculator is required to answer this question.
Consider a sample of size n=30 from a normal population where an unbiased estimate of the population variance obtained from this sample is s2=1.442. We wish to carry out a hypothesis test at a 5% significance level to determine whether there is sufficient evidence that the population variance σ2 is smaller than 2.5.

The table below shows the values χν,p such that P(X>χν,p)=p for some particular values of ν and p, where X follows a chi-square distribution χ2(ν) with ν degrees of freedom.

ν∖p 0.995 0.975 0.950 0.050 0.025 0.005
29 13.121 16.047 17.708 42.557 45.722 52.336
30 13.787 16.791 18.493 43.773 46.979 53.672
31 14.458 17.539 19.281 44.985 48.232 55.003

Using this information, select the options that make the following statements true.

The test statistic for this test is approximately
 
24.054
24.883
23.020
19.168
.

At the 5% level of significance, there is
 
insufficient
sufficient
evidence that σ2<2.5.
Submit

x―=2.36,x2―=18.62

We wish to carry out a hypothesis test at a 5% significance level to determine whether there is sufficient evidence that the population variance σ2 is greater than 10.

The table below shows the values χν,p such that P(X>χν,p)=p for some particular values of ν and p, where X follows a chi-square distribution χ2(ν) with ν degrees of freedom.

ν∖p 0.995 0.975 0.950 0.050 0.025 0.005
64 38.610 43.776 46.595 83.675 88.004 96.878
65 39.383 44.603 47.450 84.821 89.177 98.105
66 40.158 45.431 48.305 85.965 90.349 99.330

The test statistic for this test is approximately
 
65.192
84.828
68.428
89.735
.

At the 5% level of significance, there is
 
sufficient
insufficient
evidence that σ2>10.

Question 3
?
A scientific calculator is required to answer this question.
Consider a sample of size n=12 from a normal population, where an unbiased estimate for the population variance obtained from this sample is s2=32. We wish to carry out a hypothesis test at a 2% significance level to determine whether there is sufficient evidence that the population variance σ2≠4.

The table below shows the values χν,p such that P(X>χν,p)=p for some particular values of ν and p, where X follows a chi-square distribution χ2(ν) with ν degrees of freedom.

ν∖p 0.995 0.990 0.980 0.010 0.020 0.050
10 2.156 2.558 3.059 23.209 21.161 18.307
11 2.603 3.053 3.609 24.725 22.618 19.675
12 3.074 3.571 4.178 26.217 24.054 21.026

Using this information, select the options that make the following statements true.

The test statistic for this test is
 
28.64
19.11
21.32
24.75
.

At the 2% level of significance, there is
 
sufficient
insufficient
evidence that σ2≠4.

?
A scientific calculator is required to answer this question.
Consider a sample X1,X2,…,X15 of size n=15 from a normal population with the following sample data: x―=3.6,x2―=15.5 We wish to carry out a hypothesis test at a 5% significance level to determine whether there is sufficient evidence that the population variance σ2≠5.

Using the fact that P(X<5.629)=0.025 and P(X>26.119)=0.025 where X∼χ2(14) has a chi-square distribution with 14 degrees of freedom (as shown in the diagram above), complete the following reasoning.

The test statistic for this test is approximately
 
7.620
2.788
3.642
7.581
.

At the 5% level of significance, there is
 
sufficient
insufficient
evidence that σ2≠5.

Example: Conducting Hypothesis Tests for Variance: Applications
?

A hospital wants to assess if a recent staffing change has impacted the consistency of patient waiting times in the emergency department. The waiting times are known to be normally distributed, and the variance before the staffing changes was . The head of the department suspects that the variance has decreased since the staffing change. They collected data from patients and calculated an unbiased estimate for the population variance .

The table below shows the values such that for some particular values of and where follows a chi-square distribution with degrees of freedom.

Conduct a hypothesis test at significance level to determine whether sufficient evidence exists that the variance has decreased.

EXPLANATION

We are told that the population is normally distributed, but we do not know the population variance In cases like this, the random variable

follows a chi-square distribution with degrees of freedom, where

  • is the sample size,

  • is the sample variance, and

  • is the population variance.

In our example:

  • is the null hypothesis

  • is the alternative (one-tailed) hypothesis

Assuming the null hypothesis, i.e., we compute the test statistic:

Next, we find the critical region.

Since the alternative hypothesis is we must consider the left tail. This means we need to find the value such that Since

According to the table, the left-tailed critical value is

So, our critical region is Our test statistic () does not lie in the critical region, as shown below.

So, we do not reject the null hypothesis As a result, we conclude the following:

There is insufficient evidence that, at the level of significance, we have

Question 5
?
A scientific calculator is required to answer this question.
A company claims that the amounts of coffee dispensed by its coffee machine are normally distributed with variance σ2=3ml2. After routine maintenance, the owner of the coffee machine hopes that the machine has become more consistent. He decides to conduct a hypothesis test at the 10% significance level.

The owner of the coffee machine collected data from 19 cups of coffee after the maintenance and found an unbiased estimate for the population variance was s2=2.5ml2.

The table below shows the values χν,p such that P(X>χν,p)=p for some particular values of ν and p, where X follows a chi-square distribution χ2(ν) with ν degrees of freedom.

ν∖p 0.950 0.900 0.800 0.200 0.100 0.050
18 9.390 10.865 12.857 22.760 25.989 28.869
19 10.117 11.651 13.716 23.900 27.204 30.144
20 10.851 12.443 14.578 25.038 28.412 31.410

Using this information, select the options that make the following statements true.

The test statistic for this test is
 
8.5
15
9
12.5
.

At the 10% level of significance, there is
 
insufficient
sufficient
evidence that σ2<3ml2.
Submit
Question 6
?
A scientific calculator is required to answer this question.
A logistics company maintains that the weights of packages shipped to a particular region are normally distributed with variance σ2=1.6kg2. After operational changes, a logistics analyst wants to check if this variance has changed. He decides to conduct a hypothesis test at the 1% significance level.

The analyst sampled 15 packages and found an unbiased estimate for the population variance s2=4.5kg2.

The table below shows the values χν,p such that P(X>χν,p)=p for some particular values of ν and p, where X follows a chi-square distribution χ2(ν) with ν degrees of freedom.

ν∖p 0.995 0.990 0.980 0.020 0.010 0.005
13 3.565 4.107 4.765 25.472 27.688 29.819
14 4.075 4.660 5.368 26.873 29.141 31.319
15 4.601 5.229 5.985 28.259 30.578 32.801

Using this information, select the options that make the following statements true.

The test statistic for this test is
 
34.075
39.375
30.019
28.355
.

At the 1% level of significance, there is
 
sufficient
insufficient
evidence that σ2≠1.6kg2
